question_en: How do I download water level and flow data?
answer_en:
1. Go to the Download Data tab

2. Select a station by typing its name or station number in the 'select station' boxes, or scrolling through the stations in the drop-down lists

3. Select the parameter you want ('Flow' or 'Level')

4. Select the year range you want by inputting a start year and end year in the 'For years from' boxes

5. Click the 'Download Data' button to download the data as a CSV file. This file is automatically downloaded to your computer. For Apple users, you may have to enable downloads for this website.

question_en: Why can't I download the full date range I selected?
answer_en: The available year range automatically updates based on the selected station. This reflects the first and last years with recorded data. Note that this does not guarantee data exist for every year within that range as some stations may have gaps due to equipment failure, ice damage, seasonal operation, or funding changes.

question_en: Where can I find additional information to help interpret the columns, and station metadata in the downloaded CSV?
answer_en: You can view column descriptions and station details in the Quick Reference Materials section of the Download Data tab. Official station metadata is also available through the Water Survey of Canada HYDAT database.

question_en: What station information is available, and where can I find it?
answer_en: Station metadata, including station number, name, latitude, longitude, drainage area,operating schedule, variables measured, and date ranges, are available through the Water Survey of Canada HYDAT database. These metadata are also viewable from the Metadata tab.

question_en: Where can I download historical daily data?
answer_en:
Historical daily water level and discharge data for each station are available through the Water Survey of Canada HYDAT database, which is updated annually.
This Explorer provides summary values and selected data available for download.

question_en: Where can I read the full data disclaimer and terms of use?
answer_en:
See the Water Survey of Canada data terms and conditions on the Government of Canada website.
Additional terms of use specific to this Explorer are provided in the Data Disclaimer section of the Download Data tab.

question_en: How is water level measured?
answer_en: Water level (stage) is measured continuously at hydrometric stations using a combination of pressure transducers, float-driven sensors, and/or radar-based instruments. These instruments record water level at regular intervals (typically every 5 to 15 minutes) and transmit the data via satellite to the Water Survey of Canada.

question_en: How is discharge (flow) calculated?
answer_en: Discharge is not measured directly on a continuous basis. Instead, WSC hydrometric technicians make periodic manual discharge measurements using current meters or Acoustic Doppler Current Profilers (ADCPs). These measurements are used to develop a stage-discharge relationship (rating curve) for each station. Continuous discharge values are then derived by applying the rating curve to the continuously recorded water level data. In northern rivers, ice formation significantly alters the stage-discharge relationship, requiring special methods to estimate winter discharge. In general, winter water level readings are more reliable than winter discharge readings.

question_en: How often are stations visited?
answer_en: WSC hydrometric stations in the NWT are typically visited several times per year for maintenance and discharge measurements. The frequency depends on the station's priority, accessibility, and the complexity of its hydrological conditions. Remote stations may rely more heavily on satellite telemetry between visits. Many stations are operated seasonally (open water only) due to the challenges and costs of winter monitoring in northern environments.

question_en: How were hydrometric stations selected?
answer_en: Station locations are selected based on a combination of factors, including the need to monitor water levels for flood forecasting, water supply, infrastructure operations, transboundary agreement commitments, and research. Some stations have been in operation since the 1930s, while others have been established more recently to address data gaps.

question_en: What happens to monitoring during winter?
answer_en: Many NWT rivers and lakes are ice-covered for six to eight months of the year. During this period, water level monitoring continues at many stations, but discharge estimation becomes more uncertain because ice alters the relationship between water level and flow. Some stations operate only during the open-water season. Data collected during ice-affected periods require more careful interpretation due to increased uncertainty.

question_en: Why are some stations on the water level map on some days but not in others?
answer_en:
The map dynamically updates to only show stations with available data within the past 24 hours. The stations shown on the map may change from day to day depending on which gauges (stations) have recently transmitted data.

There are several reasons why a station might not appear:

  • No recent data: The station has not reported data within the past 24 hours
  • Operation schedule: Some stations are seasonal and only operate during certain times of the year
  • Station status changes: Stations may become inactive or decommissioned due to funding changes, equipment damange (e.g., from river ice), or program changes
  • No real-time capability: Some stations collect data but do not transmit it in real time (you can click on a station in the Metadata tab to view whether that station has real-time reporting capability

For the most up-to-date station status, visit Water Survey of Canada Real-Time Hydrometric Data website.

question_en: What do the different colours on the interactive map represent?
answer_en:
Colours on the interactive map indicate current discharge or water level at each station as a percentage of its respective long-term average.

  • Blue = Above average
  • Yellow = Near average
  • Red = Below average

question_en: What is the "Last Updated" date on the map?
answer_en: This date indicates when data were last added to the Explorer. Note that this is not necessarily when the Explorer itself was last updated.

question_en: What are the basin outlines on the map?
answer_en: Basins represent major drainage areas in the NWT (e.g., Mackenzie, Slave, Liard, Peel). You can toggle basin boundaries on and off using map controls by clicking the layers icon in the top right of the map.

question_en: What is discharge (flow)?
answer_en: Discharge, also referred to as streamflow or flow, is the volume of water passing through a river cross-section per unit of time. It is typically expressed in cubic metres per second (m³/s). Discharge integrates upstream contributions from rainfall, snowmelt, groundwater, and glacial melt within a given basin. These contributions vary depending on soil moisture conditions and upstream water use. Discharge is fundamental for understanding hydrological dynamics, water availability, flood risk, and ecosystem health.

question_en: What is water level (stage)?
answer_en: Water level, or stage, is the height of the water surface at a monitoring station, measured relative to a fixed and arbitrary reference point (datum). It is typically expressed in metres (m) and is not related to its height above sea level. Water level is the primary measurement recorded at hydrometric stations and is used to derive discharge through a stage-discharge rating curve.

question_en: Is water level the same as water depth?
answer_en: No. Water level (stage) is the height of the water surface measured relative to a fixed and arbitrary reference datum, and it does not indicate the depth of the river or lake. A water level of 5.0 m at a station means the water surface is 5.0 m above the station's datum, not that the water is 5.0 m deep. Water levels are useful for tracking changes over time and for flood thresholds, but they do not directly indicate water depth or navigability.

question_en: How are the data summarized on the interactive map?
answer_en: The interactive map displays current daily water level as a percentage of the long-term average for each station. Long-term averages are calculated using finalized daily data provided by the Water Survey of Canada.

question_en: Where can I find real-time water level and flow data?
answer_en: Real-time hydrometric data for NWT stations are available through the Water Survey of Canada Real-Time Hydrometric Data website. Real-time data are preliminary and subject to revision.

question_en: Where can I find related reports and datasets?
answer_en:
Historical daily data and station metadata are available through the Water Survey of Canada HYDAT database ( National Water Data Archive: HYDAT ).

Summary values, graphs and interpretation are included in GNWT-ECC publications, such as:

  • Spring Outlook Spring Water Level Outlook | Environment and Climate Change (published annually)
  • Water Monitoring Bulletins (published monthly and as needed during spring break-up)
  • State of Environment Report 11. State - Water | Environment and Climate Change (published every 5 years)

Real-time data are available via the Water Survey of Canada website (Water Level and Flow - Environment Canada).

question_en: What is the purpose of this Data Explorer?
answer_en: This Explorer is a visual and interactive tool for exploring water level and flow data from the NWT hydrometric network, operated by the Water Survey of Canada and managed in partnership with the GNWT. It allows users to explore, visualize, and download hydrometric data from stations across the NWT. The Explorer is intended to support public access to water data and complement the official WSC data holdings.
